title: Euclid's GCD
code: public static long Gcd(long a, long b)

frames.caption: gcd(48, 18). Goal: shrink the pair (a, b) toward (g, 0) where g is the answer.
frames.note: Euclid's insight: gcd(a, b) == gcd(b, a mod b), because any number dividing both a and b also divides their remainder a mod b — and vice versa. So we keep replacing the pair (a, b) with the smaller pair (b, a mod b) without ever changing the answer. The loop invariant "gcd of the current pair equals gcd of the original pair" is what makes the process correct.

frames.caption: b = 18 != 0, so keep going: there is still work to reduce.
frames.note: Euclid's insight: gcd(a, b) == gcd(b, a mod b), because any number dividing both a and b also divides their remainder a mod b — and vice versa. So we keep replacing the pair (a, b) with the smaller pair (b, a mod b) without ever changing the answer. The loop invariant "gcd of the current pair equals gcd of the original pair" is what makes the process correct.

frames.caption: r = a % b = 48 % 18 = 12. The remainder is strictly smaller than b (12 < 18).
frames.note: The mod step r = a % b is the engine of the algorithm. The remainder is always strictly smaller than b (0 <= r < b), so the pair shrinks every iteration — that is why it must terminate. The classic bound: a mod b < a / 2 whenever b <= a, so the larger value at least halves every two steps, giving O(log min(a, b)) arithmetic operations. The provable worst case is consecutive Fibonacci numbers (Lame's theorem), still logarithmic.

frames.caption: Slide the pair: a <- old b = 18, b <- r = 12. New pair (18, 12).
frames.note: Slide the window down: the old b becomes the new a, and the remainder r becomes the new b. This is the (a, b) -> (b, a mod b) move. Each row of the reviewer's trace table is exactly one of these slides, feeding the previous remainder back as the next divisor.

frames.caption: b = 12 != 0, so keep going: there is still work to reduce.
frames.note: Euclid's insight: gcd(a, b) == gcd(b, a mod b), because any number dividing both a and b also divides their remainder a mod b — and vice versa. So we keep replacing the pair (a, b) with the smaller pair (b, a mod b) without ever changing the answer. The loop invariant "gcd of the current pair equals gcd of the original pair" is what makes the process correct.

frames.caption: r = a % b = 18 % 12 = 6. The remainder is strictly smaller than b (6 < 12).
frames.note: The mod step r = a % b is the engine of the algorithm. The remainder is always strictly smaller than b (0 <= r < b), so the pair shrinks every iteration — that is why it must terminate. The classic bound: a mod b < a / 2 whenever b <= a, so the larger value at least halves every two steps, giving O(log min(a, b)) arithmetic operations. The provable worst case is consecutive Fibonacci numbers (Lame's theorem), still logarithmic.

frames.caption: Slide the pair: a <- old b = 12, b <- r = 6. New pair (12, 6).
frames.note: Slide the window down: the old b becomes the new a, and the remainder r becomes the new b. This is the (a, b) -> (b, a mod b) move. Each row of the reviewer's trace table is exactly one of these slides, feeding the previous remainder back as the next divisor.

frames.caption: b = 6 != 0, so keep going: there is still work to reduce.
frames.note: Euclid's insight: gcd(a, b) == gcd(b, a mod b), because any number dividing both a and b also divides their remainder a mod b — and vice versa. So we keep replacing the pair (a, b) with the smaller pair (b, a mod b) without ever changing the answer. The loop invariant "gcd of the current pair equals gcd of the original pair" is what makes the process correct.

frames.caption: r = a % b = 12 % 6 = 0. The remainder is strictly smaller than b (0 < 6).
frames.note: The mod step r = a % b is the engine of the algorithm. The remainder is always strictly smaller than b (0 <= r < b), so the pair shrinks every iteration — that is why it must terminate. The classic bound: a mod b < a / 2 whenever b <= a, so the larger value at least halves every two steps, giving O(log min(a, b)) arithmetic operations. The provable worst case is consecutive Fibonacci numbers (Lame's theorem), still logarithmic.

frames.caption: Slide the pair: a <- old b = 6, b <- r = 0. New pair (6, 0).
frames.note: Slide the window down: the old b becomes the new a, and the remainder r becomes the new b. This is the (a, b) -> (b, a mod b) move. Each row of the reviewer's trace table is exactly one of these slides, feeding the previous remainder back as the next divisor.

frames.caption: b == 0, so stop. gcd(48, 18) = a = 6.
frames.note: When b hits 0 the loop stops and we return a — the base case gcd(a, 0) = a. The last non-zero value we held is the greatest common divisor: 6 divides both 48 and 18, and no larger integer does. (The same identity makes gcd(0, x) = x, the right answer for empty/edge inputs.)
